stories.headline: INJURED FLUTIE OUT UNTIL THE PLAYOFFS Stars 17, Bulls 12 COLLEGE PARK, Md. (AP) -Kelvin Bryant rushed for 104 yards and scored on two short runs to pace Baltimore (9-6-1). His second score, on a 2-yard run with 10:51 left, put Baltimore in front, 17-6. Bryant also scored on a 6-yard run in the second period. Jacksonville fell to 8-7. Invaders 38 Breakers 20 OAKLAND, Calif. (AP) - Bobby Hebert passed to Anthony Carter for two touchdowns in the victory. The first went for 18 yards in the second quarter, the second for 19 in the third. It was the seventh straight victory for the Invaders (11-3-1); Portland fell to 4-11.
stories.summary: Doug Flutie suffered a broken left collarbone and will be out until the playoffs, which he may be able to join in four to six weeks. He was injured in a recent game and his team's doctor has confirmed that he will not be able to play before the playoffs.

stories.headline: HOSTAGES IN LEBANON: Dashed Plans for a Joyous Welcome;   RELEASE OF HOSTAGES PUT OFF AS LEBANESE CALL FOR PROMISE THAT U.S. WILL NOT STRIKE BACK STANDOFF IN BEIRUT
stories.summary: A plan to free 39 American hostages in Lebanon was put on hold after their captors added a new demand that the US guarantee it will not retaliate militarily after their release. The captors initially demanded the release of 735 detainees held by Israel.

stories.headline: TERRORIST LINK STILL UNCONFIRMED The Transportation Department is preparing recommendations for President Reagan on ways to improve airport security. Page A8.
stories.summary: Security measures at Canadian airports were tightened after the Air-India crash that killed 329 people, with new measures including X-ray checks for checked baggage, although officials have not yet found evidence linking the crash to terrorists. The crash occurred after a bomb exploded on a Canadian plane headed to Japan.

stories.headline: LEBANESE SHIITE THREATENS ISRAEL OF ITS SECURITY PLANS IN THE SOUTH
stories.summary: The leader of the Lebanese Shiite militia threatened to join forces with Palestinian guerrillas and carry out attacks on Israel if it keeps troops in southern Lebanon and retains a security zone north of its border. The warning came as Israel continued its pullout of combat units from Lebanon.

rounds.blurb: Doug Flutie's injury occurred as the US faced hostage and terrorism concerns. The release of American hostages in Lebanon was delayed due to new demands. Airport security measures were tightened in Canada after a deadly plane crash.

stories.headline: HUNDREDS KILLED BY WINDSTORM; The Cities of St. Louis and East St. Louis Visited by the Worst Tornado of Their History. NUMBER OF VICTIMS MAY REACH THE THOUSANDS The Cities in Absolute Darkness and Communication Cut Off -- The Money Loss High Up in Millions. MANY EXCURSION BOATS WERE LOST ON THE MISSISSIPPI RIVER. Several Missouri and Illinois Towns Reported to Have Been Wiped Out of Existence -- Two Schoolhouses Said to Have Been Demolished Causing the Loss of at Least One Hundred Lives. Flames Sweep the Wreckage.
stories.summary: A devastating tornado hit St. Louis and East St. Louis, killing hundreds of people and causing widespread destruction. The disaster also affected several nearby towns and resulted in significant financial losses.

stories.headline: MANY HUNDREDS SLAIN; Trampled to Death on the Hodynsky Plain. ACCIDENT AT CORONATION Fatal Ending of the Great Fete of the Peasants at Moscow. BARRIERS FALL BEFORE THE THRONG Over 500,000 Persons Struggling for the Free Food and Free Beer in the Great Plain. AID BY THE CZAR FOR THE INJURED Many of the Killed Peasanta Came from a Distance, and Will, Perhaps, Never Be Identified.
stories.summary: A stampede on the Hodynsky Plain in Moscow killed hundreds of people who were struggling to get free food and beer, with many more injured and some bodies possibly never identified.

stories.headline: DEATH DEALING STORMS; SOMETHING OF THE CHARACTER OF THOSE KNOWN IN THE WEST. How the Cyclone Whirls Along on Its Destructive Path, and What Sci- entific Men Say of Its Nature, Ori- gin, and Possible Cause -- Most Dis- astrons of These Disturbances Within the Past Quarter Century, and the Damage.
stories.summary: Destructive storms, similar to those known in the West, have caused significant damage and loss of life, prompting scientific investigation into their nature and origin.

stories.headline: ENEMIES OF THE COUNTRY; Repudiationists in the Senate Sup- port Bailer's Bill. THEY ATTACK THE NATION'S CREDIT Propose That No More Government Bonds Shall Be Issued Without the Consent of Congress. OUTRAGEOUS SCHEME OF SILVERITES Denounced by Senators Hill, Sher- man, Gray, Lodge, and Otters as Nothing Short of Criminal.
stories.summary: Some senators are supporting a bill that would limit the government's ability to issue bonds without congressional consent, a move that has been denounced by other senators as an attack on the nation's credit.

rounds.blurb: A devastating tornado hit St. Louis and East St. Louis, killing hundreds and causing widespread destruction. Destructive storms also occurred elsewhere, prompting scientific investigation. A stampede in Moscow killed hundreds of people struggling for free food and beer.

stories.headline: ALGERIAN DEBATE VOTED BY THE U.N.; FRENCH QUIT HALL; Motion Not to Put Question on Agenda Is Defeated in Assembly, 28 to 27 PINAY SOUNDS WARNING Says Intervention in France's Affairs Would Be Void -- Spaak Supports Him U. N. Votes to Debate on Algeria; France Declares the Action Void
stories.summary: The United Nations Assembly voted to debate the Algeria issue, despite France's opposition, and the French delegation walked out in response. The vote was a close 28 to 27, with the Asian-African bloc and the USSR bloc supporting the debate.

stories.headline: DULLES RECEIVES CONGRESS BACKING ON GENEVA PLANS; Top Leaders of Both Parties Fully Endorse His Program After Briefing Session MANY DUBIOUS ON TALKS George Says Conferees May Not Get Beyond 1st Item of Germany and Security DULLES RECEIVES CONGRESS BACKING
stories.summary: US Secretary of State Dulles received backing from Congress for his plans for the Geneva conference, but some lawmakers expressed doubts about the success of the talks. Senator George questioned whether the conference would make progress beyond the first item on the agenda, which was the reunification of Germany and security.

stories.headline: PRESIDENT POSES FOR PHOTOGRAPHS; TAKES FIRST STEPS; Tanned and Smiling, He Chats With Camera Men During an 8-Minute Session WALKS SEVERAL TIMES Wears Gay Pajamas and Tie -- Appears 'Clear, Bright as a Button' -- Enjoys Sun His Medical Staff Joins the President on Hospital Terrace for Photographs PRESIDENT POSES FOR PHOTOGRAPHS
stories.summary: The President, who had been ill, took his first steps and posed for photographs during an 8-minute session on a hospital terrace, appearing to be in good health and spirits.

stories.headline: TOP NATIVE PARTY IN MOROCCO BALKS AT JOINING REGIME; Istiqlal Calls Throne Council Illegal Because of Its Tie to Sultan ben Arafa STRIFE ERUPTS IN RABAT Formation of Cabinet Is Left in Doubt -- Paris May Face New Protectorate Crisis MOROCCAN PARTY SPURNS CABINET
stories.summary: The Istiqlal party in Morocco refused to join the government unless it was sanctioned by ben Youssef, and the party's leaders considered the Throne Council to be illegal due to its connection to Sultan ben Arafa, leading to uncertainty about the formation of a cabinet.

rounds.blurb: The United Nations Assembly voted to debate the Algeria issue, despite France's opposition. The French delegation walked out in response to the close 28 to 27 vote. The US Secretary of State received congressional backing for his Geneva conference plans.
